metadata.title: Exponent Rules — Why a to the Zero Equals One

beats.narration_text: Here is why Exponent Rules — Why a to the Zero Equals One belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

props.command: Exponent Rules — Why a to the Zero Equals One
props.segment: Exponent Rules — Why a to the Zero Equals One
output: Why a to the zero equals one

beats.narration_text: Students memorize a-to-the-zero equals one without understanding why. It is not a definition. It follows directly from the quotient rule: any nonzero number divided by itself equals one, and subtracting exponents gives you the zeroth power. The rule derives the fact.

claude — write a Manim scene proving a to the zero equals 1 using the quotient rule: show a cubed over a cubed equals a to the 3 minus 3 equals a to the zero and also equals 1, then animate all six exponent rules

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene proving a^0=1
  using the quotient rule:
  show a^3/a^3 = a^(3-3) = a^0 and also =1
  then animate all six exponent rules"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes it. exponent_rules.py — prints the step-by-step proof that a cubed over a cubed equals 1, then lists all six exponent rules: product, quotient, power, zero exponent, negative exponent, and fractional exponent.

props.code:
# exponent_rules.py — Claude Code output
a = 2
print(f"a^3 / a^3 = {a**3}/{a**3} = {a**3//a**3}")
print(f"a^(3-3) = a^0")
print(f"Therefore a^0 = 1")
RULES = ["a^m·a^n=a^(m+n)", "a^m/a^n=a^(m-n)",
         "(a^m)^n=a^(mn)", "a^0=1",
         "a^(-n)=1/a^n", "a^(1/n)=nth_root(a)"]
for r in RULES: print(r)
props.title: exponent_rules.py

beats.narration_text: Run it. The proof unfolds in four steps: start with a cubed over a cubed. Apply quotient rule to get a to the 3 minus 3. Note the same expression equals 1. Therefore a to the zero equals 1. Gold box confirms the result. Caveat: zero to the zero is indeterminate.
claude — add a worked example for each rule using base 2 with specific numbers

props.command:
claude "add a worked example for each rule
  using base 2 with specific numbers"

beats.narration_text: All six rules with base-2 examples. Product: 2 cubed times 2 squared equals 32. Quotient: 2 to the fifth over 2 squared equals 8. Power: quantity 2 cubed squared equals 64. Zero: 7 to the zero equals 1. Negative: 2 to the negative 3 equals one eighth. Fractional: 8 to the one third equals 2.

The zero exponent rule is not special — it is what you get when you apply the quotient rule consistently. The negative exponent rule is the same: extend the pattern below zero and you get reciprocals. Mathematics is internally consistent; memorization is a shortcut that hides the logic.

Derive the power rule from scratch using repeated application of the product rule. Write out quantity a to the m end quantity to the n as n copies of a to the m and count the total exponents. You will get a to the mn without using any formula.
